\makegloss

A Global Game with Heterogenous Priors111I thank Martin Hellwig for bringing the literature on global games to my attention. I thank Sophie Bade, Nataliya Demchenko, Alia Gizatulina, Olga Gorelkina, Dominik Grafenhofer, Carl Christian von Weizsï¿½cker, and seminar participants in Bonn for helpful and encouraging discussions.

Wolfgang Kuhle

Max Planck Institute for Research on Collective Goods, Kurt-Schumacher-Str. 10, 53113 Bonn, Germany. Email: kuhle@coll.mpg.de.

Abstract: This paper relaxes the common prior assumption in the public and private information game of Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004). For the generalized game, where the agent’s prior expectations are heterogenous, it derives a sharp condition for the emergence of unique/multiple equilibria. This condition indicates that unique equilibria are played if player’s public disagreement is substantial. If disagreement is small, equilibrium multiplicity depends on the relative precisions of private signals and subjective priors. Extensions to environments with public signals of exogenous and endogenous quality show that prior heterogeneity, unlike heterogeneity in private information, provides a robust anchor for unique equilibria. Finally, irrespective of whether priors are common or not, we show that public signals can ensure equilibrium uniqueness, rather than multiplicity, if they are sufficiently precise.
Keywords: Global Games, Equilibrium Selection, Heterogenous Priors, Thin out Effect
JEL: D53, D82, D83

1 Introduction

Heterogeneity in private beliefs is the core component of global coordination games. In the original two-player games introduced by Carlsson and van Damme, (1993) and Rubinstein, (1989), as well as in the Morris and Shin, (1998) extension with a continuum of players, it is a small perturbation away from common knowledge, which selects unique equilibria. This pivotal role of private beliefs was put into perspective by Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004), Hellwig, (2002), and Metz, (2002), who introduce common priors and public signals into the global games model. In such extended settings, it turns out that the global game structure, and its inductive equilibrium selection mechanism, is distorted. And it depends on the relative precision of public and private information whether agents can coordinate on multiple equilibria.

Given that the dispersion of private beliefs is pivotal to the global games approach, it is complementary to study how a relaxation of the common prior assumption, which adds a new dimension to the dispersion of private beliefs, affects equilibrium outcomes. To address this question, we study coordination games which are played by a continuum of agents that differ with regard to (i) private information and (ii) the mean of their prior over an unknown fundamental that characterizes the game’s payoffs. While the heterogeneity in private signals is unobservable to players, players will know the distribution of priors. That is, we study how agents can coordinate on equilibria in environments, where they know that others hold, and act upon, views that they commonly know to disagree with. The main result shows that if player’s public disagreement regarding the coordination game’s payoff structure is substantial, then they play unique equilibria. That is, unique equilibria can be selected through the dispersion of priors.

We derive our results in two steps. First, we isolate the pure interaction between private signals and dispersed priors in an environment which abstracts from public signals. Second, we add three different types of public signals to examine the robustness of our results. In the first environment, which abstracts from public signals, we find that heterogeneity in beliefs, which originates from the dispersion of prior expectations μ𝜇\mu, contributes to equilibrium uniqueness. Dispersion in private signals, on the contrary, induces equilibrium multiplicity. In the extended settings, where we introduce different types of public signals, we find that prior heterogeneity still unambiguously contributes towards equilibrium uniqueness. The role of private signals, on the contrary, is ambiguous and depends critically on the specification of the public signal. The main finding in the second part of the paper is therefore that heterogeneity in priors, unlike heterogeneity in private information, provides a robust anchor for unique equilibria. Put differently, we show that heterogeneous priors robustly select unique equilibria in rather diverse environments. In turn, we compare this finding to the literature where the presence of common priors induces multiple rather than unique equilibria.

Finally, as a byproduct of our analysis, we find that public signals in themselves, irrespective of whether priors are heterogenous or common, have an ambiguous effect on equilibrium multiplicity: increases in the public signal’s precision can ensure equilibrium uniqueness. This result is of interest in comparison with those of Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004), Hellwig, (2002), Metz, (2002), Angeletos and Werning, (2006) who find that increases in the public signal’s precision unambiguously induce multiple rather than unique equilibria.222There will be two classes of equilibria in this setting, and the public signal’s precision ensures uniqueness for every given signal realization. However, if the public signal’s precision is sufficiently high, there may be multiplicity in the public signal’s realization itself.

Compared to the literature, we extend the canonic common prior coordination games by Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004) to a setting where agents’ prior distribution’s mean μ𝜇\mu, regarding the true state of the world, are normally distributed over the economy’s population. In the model of Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004), equilibrium uniqueness is ensured iff αxαp≥12​πsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝12𝜋\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}; where αxsubscript𝛼𝑥\alpha_{x} is the private signal’s precision and αpsubscript𝛼𝑝\alpha_{p} is the prior’s precision. For the generalized game, where subjective prior expectations μ𝜇\mu are distributed with variance σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}, we obtain (αxαp)2+σμ2≥12​πsuperscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇12𝜋\sqrt{(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}+\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} as an analog condition for equilibrium uniqueness. This formula’s main strength is that it isolates how prior heterogeneity σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu} affects the number of possible equilibria. In general, the condition shows that incomplete information games where the population of players is polarized such that there are two large tails have unique equilibria. Or, equivalently, unique equilibria are ensured if there is only a small group of agents which have “intermediate” or “moderate” beliefs. Put differently, increases in the priors dispersion, σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}, “thin out” the group of agents with “moderate” beliefs which can, potentially, coordinate on multiple equilibria. Technically, equilibrium multiplicity depends on the relative precisions of private information (αx)subscript𝛼𝑥(\alpha_{x}) and the subjective prior (αp)subscript𝛼𝑝(\alpha_{p}) if the prior’s dispersion is small (σμ<12​π)subscript𝜎𝜇12𝜋(\sigma_{\mu}<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}). For sufficiently dispersed priors, (σμ>12​π)subscript𝜎𝜇12𝜋(\sigma_{\mu}>\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}), equilibria are unique, irrespective of the relative precisions of private signals and priors. The original uniqueness condition for the common prior economy (αxαp≥12​π)subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝12𝜋(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}) obtains as prior dispersion (σμ)subscript𝜎𝜇(\sigma_{\mu}) vanishes.

In Section 3, we extend the information structure and embed our coordination game into three different public signal environments. These extensions provide a background to study the fundamental difference between heterogeneity in priors and private signals. The main finding in this section is that only heterogeneity in priors selects unique equilibria reliably. On the contrary, the role of both private and public signals changes from environment to environment. These findings originate, first of all, from an environment where the public signal reveals the true state of the game with exogenous quality; secondly, from an environment that may be seen as a financial markets context, where stock prices aggregate and reveal dispersed private information with endogenous quality; and thirdly, from an environment with public signals that allow agents to observe each other’s actions. Comparison of all three public signal settings shows that the prior’s dispersion σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu} is the only parameter which can ensure unique equilibria in all environments. The implications of the public and private signals’ precisions, however, vary from case to case. In particular, increases in the private signal’s precision ensure uniqueness (multiplicity) when public signals are of exogenous (endogenous) precision.

Recently, Steiner and Stewart, (2008), Izmalkov and Yildiz, (2010), and Mathevet, (2012) have introduced heterogenous priors into global games. Steiner and Stewart, (2008), Izmalkov and Yildiz, (2010), and Mathevet, (2012) focus, respectively, on learning, rationalizability of strategies, and applications to mechanism design. Unlike Steiner and Stewart, (2008) and Mathevet, (2012), who focus on N-player games, we study games with a continuum of players as in Izmalkov and Yildiz, (2010). Contrary to Izmalkov and Yildiz, (2010), who focus on the private information limit333In the “private information limit”, the private signal’s precision goes to infinity. A crucial consequence of this is that the importance of priors for the agent’s decisions vanishes. of Carlsson and van Damme, (1993), which carries over to these more general games, the present paper develops a sharp characterization of equilibrium multiplicity at and away from the private information limit.444Izmalkov and Yildiz, (2010) discuss prior heterogeneity in a game with a continuum of players. Izmalkov and Yildiz, (2010), p. 25, focus on the formation of individual threshold strategies and avoid the “delicate” question of equilibrium multiplicity, which is the focus of the present paper. More precisely, Izmalkov and Yildiz, (2010) study games with heterogenous priors (“sentiments”) and characterize for a family of coordination games how prior dispersion affects the unique threshold equilibrium which is ensured once private information becomes sufficiently precise. Due to the focus on the private information limit, it remains an open question how prior heterogeneity affects the number of equilibria in those cases where private signals are not arbitrarily precise. This question is the focus of the current paper. Finally, while Izmalkov and Yildiz, (2010) characterize games with general distribution functions, the present characterization of equilibrium uniqueness and multiplicity, away from the limit where private information is very precise, relies on the afore mentioned normality assumptions. The present paper is therefore the first to derive closed form expressions for the emergence of multiple equilibria in large games with heterogeneous priors. Moreover, by means of the public signal environments, it provides a framework to interpret the role of prior heterogeneity in the global games structure.555Steiner and Stewart, (2008), Izmalkov and Yildiz, (2010), and Mathevet, (2012) abstract from public signals.

Regarding the heterogeneity in priors, we note that Morris and Shin, (1998, 2000, 2004) embed their common prior incomplete information coordination games in a currency crises, bank-run and debt-run context respectively. In these contexts, our assumption of a prior distribution μi∼𝒩​(E​[μ],σμ2)similar-tosubscript𝜇𝑖𝒩𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇\mu_{i}\sim\mathcal{N}(E[\mu],\sigma^{2}_{\mu}), which is known to all agents, may be interpreted as a publicly observable distribution of exchange rate forecasts or credit ratings. Such a distribution of conflicting beliefs, may also be seen as consistent with publicly disclosed long and short positions that large investors take in a firm’s stock or debt. While Morris, (1995) and Sethi and Yildiz, (2012), discuss heterogenous priors and their origins in detail, we will take prior heterogeneity as a given. In turn, we focus purely on the implications that public disagreement has for the emergence of multiple equilibria in the global games framework.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2.1, we introduce the model. Section 2.2 contains the main result. In Section 3, we reflect on our findings in three distinct public signal environments. Section 4 concludes.

2 The Model

To isolate the implications of prior dispersion, we start with a setting that differs only with regard to the heterogeneity of priors from the canonic framework introduced by Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004).

2.1 Strategies, Payoffs and Information

There is a status quo and a unit measure of agents indexed by i∈[0,1]𝑖01i\in[0,1]. Each of these agents i𝑖i can choose between two actions ai∈{0,1}subscript𝑎𝑖01a_{i}\in\{0,1\}. Choosing ai=1subscript𝑎𝑖1a_{i}=1 means to attack the status quo. Choosing ai=0subscript𝑎𝑖0a_{i}=0 means that the agent does not attack the status quo. An attack on the status quo is associated with a cost c∈(0,1)𝑐01c\in(0,1). If the attack is successful, the status quo is abandoned and attacking agents receive a net payoff 1−c>01𝑐01-c>0. If the attack is not successful, an attacking agent’s net payoff is −c𝑐-c. The payoff for an agent who does not attack is normalized to zero.

The status quo is abandoned if the aggregate size of the attack A:=∫01ai​𝑑iassign𝐴superscriptsubscript01subscript𝑎𝑖differential-d𝑖A:=\int_{0}^{1}a_{i}di exceeds the strength of the status quo θ𝜃\theta, i.e., if A>θ𝐴𝜃A>\theta. Otherwise, if A<θ𝐴𝜃A<\theta, the status quo is maintained and the attack fails. Regarding the fundamental, θ𝜃\theta, each agent i𝑖i holds a prior belief θ∼𝒩​(μi,σp2)similar-to𝜃𝒩subscript𝜇𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝑝\theta\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu_{i},\sigma^{2}_{p}). In our setting, we assume that the priors, regarding the fundamental θ𝜃\theta, are normally distributed across the population, i.e., μi∼𝒩​(E​[μ],σμ2)similar-tosubscript𝜇𝑖𝒩𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇\mu_{i}\sim\mathcal{N}(E[\mu],\sigma^{2}_{\mu}). And the distribution of priors is common knowledge. In addition to the prior, each agent receives a private signal xi=θ+σx​ξisubscript𝑥𝑖𝜃subscript𝜎𝑥subscript𝜉𝑖x_{i}=\theta+\sigma_{x}\xi_{i}. Where signal noise ξi∼𝒩​(0,1)similar-tosubscript𝜉𝑖𝒩01\xi_{i}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1) is i.i.d. across the population. We parameterize the information structure in terms of precisions αx≡1σx2subscript𝛼𝑥1superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑥2\alpha_{x}\equiv\frac{1}{\sigma_{x}^{2}} and αp≡1σp2subscript𝛼𝑝1superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑝2\alpha_{p}\equiv\frac{1}{\sigma_{p}^{2}}. Agents use their information to calculate expected utility

E​[U​(A,θ,c,ai)|μi,xi]=ai​(1×P​(θ​<A|​μi,xi)−c).𝐸delimited-[]conditional𝑈𝐴𝜃𝑐subscript𝑎𝑖subscript𝜇𝑖subscript𝑥𝑖subscript𝑎𝑖1𝑃𝜃bra𝐴subscript𝜇𝑖subscript𝑥𝑖𝑐\displaystyle E[U(A,\theta,c,a_{i})|\mu_{i},x_{i}]=a_{i}(1\times P(\theta<A|\mu_{i},x_{i})-c). (1)

Where P​(θ​<A|​μi,xi)𝑃𝜃bra𝐴subscript𝜇𝑖subscript𝑥𝑖P(\theta<A|\mu_{i},x_{i}) is the probability that the attack is successful given signal xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i} and prior belief μisubscript𝜇𝑖\mu_{i}. Regarding payoffs, (1) reflects that an agent i𝑖i, who does not attack (ai=0)subscript𝑎𝑖0(a_{i}=0) receives a safe payoff of 00, while an agent who does attack (ai=1)subscript𝑎𝑖1(a_{i}=1) receives 1−c1𝑐1-c if the attack is successful, and −c𝑐-c otherwise. To close the description of agents’ information, we note that the distributions of xisubscript𝑥𝑖x_{i} and μisubscript𝜇𝑖\mu_{i} are commonly known to all players. With the private choice problem in place, we now characterize equilibrium.

2.2 Equilibrium

As Morris and Shin, (1998, 2004), we focus on monotone threshold equilibria. Each threshold equilibrium will be characterized by a pair ψ∗,θ∗superscript𝜓superscript𝜃\psi^{*},\theta^{*}. Where θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*} separates values θ<θ∗𝜃superscript𝜃\theta<\theta^{*} for which the status quo is abandoned from values θ>θ∗𝜃superscript𝜃\theta>\theta^{*} where it prevails. The threshold level ψ∗≡αxα​x∗+αpα​μ∗superscript𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼superscript𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼superscript𝜇\psi^{*}\equiv\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}x^{*}+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\mu^{*} summarizes the critical pairs x∗,μ∗superscript𝑥superscript𝜇x^{*},\mu^{*}, for which agents are just indifferent between attacking and not attacking.666In the context of the currency crises model of Morris and Shin, (1998), attacking agents would sell short a country’s currency, and the central bank’s reserves θ𝜃\theta are either sufficient (θ>A)𝜃𝐴(\theta>A) to defend the peg or not (θ<A)𝜃𝐴(\theta<A). In another interpretation, agents can run on/sell short a firm’s debt, and if the firm’s financial strength is insufficient it defaults. The equilibrium pairs, ψ∗,θ∗superscript𝜓superscript𝜃\psi^{*},\theta^{*}, are determined by the simultaneous evaluation of the payoff indifference condition and the critical mass condition. The payoff indifference condition, PIC, follows directly from the individual choice problem (1). Taking the threshold level θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*} as given, we have:777See Raiffa and Schlaifer, (2000), p. 250, for the standard results on prior and posterior distributions of normally distributed variables which are used throughout the paper.888Note that we already use the (forthcoming) critical mass condition, (4), which requires that θ∗≡A​(ψ∗,θ∗)superscript𝜃𝐴superscript𝜓superscript𝜃\theta^{*}\equiv A(\psi^{*},\theta^{*}), and replace A𝐴A with θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*} in (1) to obtain (2).

P​(θ≤θ∗|x∗,μ∗)=c⇔Φ​(α​(θ∗−αxα​x∗−αpα​μ∗))=c;α=αx+αp,formulae-sequence𝑃𝜃conditionalsuperscript𝜃superscript𝑥superscript𝜇𝑐⇔formulae-sequenceΦ𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼superscript𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼superscript𝜇𝑐𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝\displaystyle P(\theta\leq\theta^{*}|x^{*},\mu^{*})=c\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha}(\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}x^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\mu^{*})\Big{)}=c;\quad\alpha=\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}, (2)

where Φ​()Φ\Phi() is the cumulative of the standard normal distribution. The PIC (2) characterizes those pairs x∗,μ∗superscript𝑥superscript𝜇x^{*},\mu^{*} which are such that the agent is indifferent between attacking and not attacking. To work with Condition (2), we define ψ≡αxα​x+αpα​μ𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝜇\psi\equiv\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}x+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\mu such that (2) writes:

P​(θ≤θ∗|ψ∗)=c⇔Φ​(α​(θ∗−ψ∗))=c;α=αx+αp.formulae-sequence𝑃𝜃conditionalsuperscript𝜃superscript𝜓𝑐⇔formulae-sequenceΦ𝛼superscript𝜃superscript𝜓𝑐𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝\displaystyle P(\theta\leq\theta^{*}|\psi^{*})=c\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha}(\theta^{*}-\psi^{*})\Big{)}=c;\quad\alpha=\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}. (3)

And agents who receive ψ≤ψ∗𝜓superscript𝜓\psi\leq\psi^{*}, which is evidence of a weak fundamental, attack. Agents who receive ψ>ψ∗𝜓superscript𝜓\psi>\psi^{*} do not attack since they believe in a strong fundamental, which makes a successful attack unlikely.

The critical mass condition, CMC, takes the cutoff value ψ∗superscript𝜓\psi^{*} as given and determines the threshold θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*}, where the attack is just strong enough to overwhelm the status quo. To calculate the mass of attacking agents, we note that ψ|θ∼𝒩​(αxα​θ+αpα​E​[μ],αψ−1)similar-toconditional𝜓𝜃𝒩subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇superscriptsubscript𝛼𝜓1\psi|\theta\sim\mathcal{N}(\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu],\alpha_{\psi}^{-1}) where αψ≡α2αx+αp2​σμ2subscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝛼2subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇\alpha_{\psi}\equiv\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}} and α=αx+αp𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝\alpha=\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}. The CMC therefore writes:

A​(ψ∗,θ∗)≡P​(ψ​<ψ∗|​θ∗)=θ∗⇔Φ​(αψ​(ψ∗−αxα​θ∗−αpα​E​[μ]))=θ∗.formulae-sequence𝐴superscript𝜓superscript𝜃𝑃𝜓brasuperscript𝜓superscript𝜃superscript𝜃⇔Φsubscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇superscript𝜃\displaystyle A(\psi^{*},\theta^{*})\equiv P(\psi<\psi^{*}|\theta^{*})=\theta^{*}\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}(\psi^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu])\Big{)}=\theta^{*}. (4)

Again, Φ​()Φ\Phi() is the cumulative of the standard normal distribution. Regarding (4), we note that it implies that there exists only one ψ∗superscript𝜓\psi^{*} for every θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*}. Simultaneous evaluation of (3) and (4) yields threshold equilibria ψ∗,θ∗superscript𝜓superscript𝜃\psi^{*},\theta^{*}:

Proposition 1.

The equilibrium ψ∗,θ∗superscript𝜓superscript𝜃\psi^{*},\theta^{*} is unique, for all parameter pairs, if and only if
(αxαp)2+σμ2≥12​πsuperscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇12𝜋\sqrt{(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}+\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}.

Proof.

We solve (3) for the threshold level ψ∗=−Φ−1​(c)​1α+θ∗superscript𝜓superscriptΦ1𝑐1𝛼superscript𝜃\psi^{*}=-\Phi^{-1}(c)\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}+\theta^{*}, and substitute ψ∗superscript𝜓\psi^{*} into (4) to obtain a one-dimensional equation in θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*}:

Φ​(αψ​(−Φ−1​(c)​1α+θ∗−αxα​θ∗−αpα​E​[μ]))=θ∗.Φsubscript𝛼𝜓superscriptΦ1𝑐1𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇superscript𝜃\displaystyle\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}\Big{(}-\Phi^{-1}(c)\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}+\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu]\Big{)}\Big{)}=\theta^{*}. (5)

The sufficient condition for uniqueness of the threshold θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*} is therefore:

αψ​αpα​ϕ​(αψ​(−Φ−1​(c)​1α+αpα​θ∗−αpα​E​[μ]))≦1subscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼italic-ϕsubscript𝛼𝜓superscriptΦ1𝑐1𝛼subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇1\displaystyle\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}\Big{(}-\Phi^{-1}(c)\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu]\Big{)}\Big{)}\leqq 1
⇔αψ​αpα​12​π​e−(αψ​(−Φ−1​(c)​1α+αpα​θ∗−αpα​E​[μ]))2​12≦1.⇔absentsubscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼12𝜋superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝛼𝜓superscriptΦ1𝑐1𝛼subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇2121\displaystyle\Leftrightarrow\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}\Big{(}-\Phi^{-1}(c)\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu]\Big{)}\Big{)}^{2}\frac{1}{2}}\leqq 1. (6)

Finally, we recall that αψ=α2αx+αp2​σμ2subscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝛼2subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇\alpha_{\psi}=\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}} and take logarithms to obtain:

l​n​(12​π​1(αxαp)2+σμ2)≦(αψ​(−Φ−1​(c)​1α+αpα​θ∗−αpα​E​[μ]))2​12.𝑙𝑛12𝜋1superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇superscriptsubscript𝛼𝜓superscriptΦ1𝑐1𝛼subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇212\displaystyle ln\Big{(}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}+\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}}\Big{)}\leqq\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}\Big{(}-\Phi^{-1}(c)\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu]\Big{)}\Big{)}^{2}\frac{1}{2}.

Accordingly, unique equilibria are ensured once:999That is, if (7) holds, then (6) never holds with equality for real-valued θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*}’s. Put differently, the polynomial which characterizes those values θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*}, for which (6) holds with equality, has two complex roots.

l​n​(12​π​1(αxαp)2+σμ2)≤0⇔(αxαp)2+σμ2≥12​π.formulae-sequence𝑙𝑛12𝜋1superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇0⇔superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇12𝜋\displaystyle ln\Big{(}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}+\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}}\Big{)}\leq 0\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}}\Big{)}^{2}+\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}. (7)

∎

In one interpretation, the role of the prior’s dispersion σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu} in Condition (7) indicates that polarized economies, with two large groups (tails) which believe that the status quo is going to be maintained (abandoned), have unique equilibria. Put differently, increases in the prior’s dispersion σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu} “thin out” the group of agents, around the mean E​[μ]𝐸delimited-[]𝜇E[\mu] who hold “moderate” beliefs. And it is this group which can potentially coordinate on multiple equilibria. Regarding the prior’s weight, αpsubscript𝛼𝑝\alpha_{p}, we find that, for every given prior expectation μ𝜇\mu, increases in αpsubscript𝛼𝑝\alpha_{p} make actions more predictable. This allows agents to coordinate, as in the common prior economy of Morris and Shin, (2004), which contributes towards equilibrium multiplicity. Comparison of the two origins of belief heterogeneity indicates that increased dispersion of private signals contributes towards equilibrium multiplicity, while increases in the dispersion of prior beliefs μ𝜇\mu contributes towards uniqueness. In the next section, we show that the thinning-out effect is robust to various changes in the informational environment. And it is the only avenue that can ensure unique equilibria in all three public signal environments that follow.

From a technical perspective, we note that Proposition 1 has two corollaries:

Corollary 1.

If the prior μ𝜇\mu is sufficiently dispersed, such that σμ>12​πsubscript𝜎𝜇12𝜋\sigma_{\mu}>\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}, equilibria are unique irrespective of the relative precision αxαpsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}} of private signal and prior. And in the case where the private signal is uninformative, αx=0subscript𝛼𝑥0\alpha_{x}=0, unique (multiple) equilibria exist if σμ>(<)​12​πsubscript𝜎𝜇12𝜋\sigma_{\mu}>(<)\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}.

Corollary 2.

The uniqueness condition (αxαp)2+σμ2≥12​πsuperscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇12𝜋\sqrt{(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}+\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} converges smoothly to the uniqueness condition, αxαp≥12​πsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝12𝜋\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}, of the Morris and Shin, (2004) common prior game, as σμ→0→subscript𝜎𝜇0\sigma_{\mu}\rightarrow 0.

3 Public Signals

To reflect on the role of prior heterogeneity, we introduce three different types of public signals into our baseline setting. Each of these public signals is chosen to isolate particular differences between heterogeneity in priors and heterogeneity in private signals. The main finding in this section is that only heterogeneity in priors selects unique equilibria reliably. That is, if player’s disagreement, as measured by σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}, is substantial, then they play unique equilibria irrespective of the particular public signal context. That is, the result from the previous section, i.e., that prior heterogeneity induces equilibrium uniqueness through the thinning-out effect, is robust to the introduction of public signals. On the contrary, the role the private signal will change from environment to environment.

The first public signal, which we introduce into our baseline model from Section 2, is of exogenous quality. That is, it reveals the true fundamental of the game with exogenous precision αzsubscript𝛼𝑧\alpha_{z}. In such an extended setting, we find that the comparative statics of the subjective prior’s precision αpsubscript𝛼𝑝\alpha_{p} change: contrary to the baseline setting, where increases in the prior weight always contribute towards multiplicity, we find that increases in the prior weight can now shift the modified economy from multiplicity towards uniqueness. In a second step, we endogenize the quality of the public signal. To do so, we embed our coordination game into a financial markets context, where a stock price aggregates and reveals dispersed private information. In this setting, we find that the role of private information, with respect to equilibrium multiplicity, is reversed. Namely, equilibrium multiplicity is ensured in the limit where private information becomes arbitrarily precise. On the contrary, the role of prior dispersion is robust to such changes in the model structure.

In the last Section 3.1, we introduce a public signal which partially reveals the aggregate attack A𝐴A. This signal provides an environment where changes in the prior’s dispersion may, for intermediate values, induce multiplicity rather than uniqueness. However, in the limit where prior dispersion grows large, it still ensures unique equilibria. Finally, as a byproduct of our analysis in Section 3.1, we find that public signals in themselves have an ambiguous effect on equilibrium multiplicity: sufficiently precise public signals can ensure unique threshold equilibria. This finding is of independent interest in comparison with the games of Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004), Hellwig, (2002), Metz, (2002), Angeletos and Werning, (2006), where increases in the public signal’s precision unambiguously induce multiple rather than unique threshold equilibria.

1) Public Signal with Exogenous Precision

The public signal

Z=θ+σz​ε,ε∼𝒩​(0,1),formulae-sequence𝑍𝜃subscript𝜎𝑧𝜀similar-to𝜀𝒩01\displaystyle Z=\theta+\sigma_{z}\varepsilon,\quad\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1), (8)

allows agents to forecast the true state of the fundamental with precision αz=1σz2subscript𝛼𝑧1superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑧2\alpha_{z}=\frac{1}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}. Agents can therefore use Z𝑍Z, in addition to x𝑥x and μ𝜇\mu, to calculate the probability with which the aggregate attack overwhelms the status quo. In Appendix A, we show that, if this signal is used as an additional source of information in the coordination game of Section 2, we have:

Proposition 2.

The equilibrium in the public and private information game with heterogenous priors is unique if

(αxαp+αz)2+σμ2​1(1+αzαp)2≥12​π.superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇1superscript1subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝212𝜋\displaystyle\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}}\Big{)}^{2}+\sigma^{2}_{\mu}\frac{1}{(1+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}. (9)

In particular, if the prior’s dispersion is large, such that σμ​11+αzαp>12​πsubscript𝜎𝜇11subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝12𝜋\sigma_{\mu}\frac{1}{1+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{p}}}>\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}, the equilibrium is unique independently of the private signal’s precision αxsubscript𝛼𝑥\alpha_{x}.

Compared to the uniqueness condition (7) from the baseline model, we find once again that the modified condition (9) has two elements. The first, αxαp+αzsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}}, reflects the trade-off between private information αxsubscript𝛼𝑥\alpha_{x} and prior αpsubscript𝛼𝑝\alpha_{p}, described by Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004) in an economy without public signals, or respectively, the trade-off between private information and public signals αzsubscript𝛼𝑧\alpha_{z} which was emphasized by Metz, (2002) and Hellwig, (2002) in an economy with a uniform uninformative prior. Regarding this first term, we find that public information and prior are perfect substitutes, and both contribute to equilibrium multiplicity. The second term σμ2​1(1+αzαp)2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇1superscript1subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝2\sigma^{2}_{\mu}\frac{1}{(1+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}}, however, shows that increases in the public signal’s precision αzsubscript𝛼𝑧\alpha_{z} reduce the effect of the prior’s dispersion, while the prior weight αpsubscript𝛼𝑝\alpha_{p} increases it. Condition (9) therefore shows that the public signal’s precision unambiguously contributes towards multiplicity. Increases in the prior’s weight αpsubscript𝛼𝑝\alpha_{p} on the contrary have an ambiguous consequences as they shift the economy towards uniqueness (multiplicity) if σμ2>(<)​αxαp​αzsubscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧\sigma^{2}_{\mu}>(<)\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha_{p}\alpha_{z}}. Finally, (9) reflects that equilibria are unique in the private information limit where αx→∞→subscript𝛼𝑥\alpha_{x}\rightarrow\infty.

2) Public Signal with Endogenous Precision

To highlight the different implications of prior dispersion and the dispersion of private signals, we discuss an environment where the global game is embedded in a financial market setting. Following, Atkeson, (2000), Angeletos and Werning, (2006), and Hellwig et al., (2006), we introduce a financial market which aggregates dispersed private information on the unknown fundamental θ𝜃\theta, through its publicly observable stock price, as in Grossman and Stiglitz, (1976, 1980), and Hellwig, (1980).101010That is, we assume that agents trade stocks prior to the coordination game. These stocks are traded at a market price P𝑃P and pay an unknown amount θ𝜃\theta. This market price will, in equilibrium, aggregate dispersed private information and reveal the true fundamental θ𝜃\theta partially. Where the partial revelation is due to aggregate noise-trader activity, σε​εsubscript𝜎𝜀𝜀\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon, ε∼𝒩​(0,1)similar-to𝜀𝒩01\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1), on the asset’s supply side. In one interpretation, the extended model may describe a situation, where bond investors use a firm’s stock price to infer its default probability, which is of importance for a coordination game that concerns a potential run on the firm’s debt. We show in Appendix B that it is possible to specify the financial market such that the public stock price signal, Z𝑍Z, partially reveals the true fundamental θ𝜃\theta:

Z=θ−γ​σε​σx2​ε,ε∼𝒩​(0,1).formulae-sequence𝑍𝜃𝛾subscript𝜎𝜀subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝑥𝜀similar-to𝜀𝒩01\displaystyle Z=\theta-\gamma\sigma_{\varepsilon}\sigma^{2}_{x}\varepsilon,\quad\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1). (10)

Thus, the signal’s precision αz=1(γ​σε)2​αx2subscript𝛼𝑧1superscript𝛾subscript𝜎𝜀2subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑥\alpha_{z}=\frac{1}{(\gamma\sigma_{\varepsilon})^{2}}\alpha^{2}_{x} is an increasing function of the private signal’s precision αx=1σx2subscript𝛼𝑥1superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑥2\alpha_{x}=\frac{1}{\sigma_{x}^{2}}. That is, the stock price’s informativeness increases once the stock investors’ information becomes more informative. In the current context, it is important that the precision with which this financial market publicly reveals the true state of the world θ𝜃\theta is increasing faster (in the private signal’s precision) than the private signal’s precision αxsubscript𝛼𝑥\alpha_{x} itself. To perform the equilibrium analysis which concerns the coordination game, we recall (9) and note that αz=αz​(αx)subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥\alpha_{z}=\alpha_{z}(\alpha_{x}). This yields

Proposition 3.

The equilibrium is unique if

(αxαp+αz​(αx))2+σμ2​1(1+αz​(αx)αp)2≥12​π,αz:=1(γ​σε)2​αx2.formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇1superscript1subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝212𝜋assignsubscript𝛼𝑧1superscript𝛾subscript𝜎𝜀2subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑥\displaystyle\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}(\alpha_{x})}\Big{)}^{2}+\sigma^{2}_{\mu}\frac{1}{(1+\frac{\alpha_{z}(\alpha_{x})}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}},\quad\alpha_{z}:=\frac{1}{(\gamma\sigma_{\varepsilon})^{2}}\alpha^{2}_{x}. (11)

Multiple equilibria exist in the private information limit where αx→∞→subscript𝛼𝑥\alpha_{x}\rightarrow\infty. The equilibrium is unique in the limit where σμ→∞→subscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}\rightarrow\infty.

Proof.

Follows from (11) with αz​(αx)=1(γ​σε)2​αx2subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥1superscript𝛾subscript𝜎𝜀2subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑥\alpha_{z}(\alpha_{x})=\frac{1}{(\gamma\sigma_{\varepsilon})^{2}}\alpha^{2}_{x}.∎

Proposition 3 establishes that the finding of Angeletos and Werning, (2006) carries over to an economy with heterogenous priors. Namely, if stock prices aggregate private information rapidly as in (10), then it is precise private information which ensures equilibrium multiplicity. Moreover, as αz​(αx)subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥\alpha_{z}(\alpha_{x}) becomes large, it marginalizes the influence of prior heterogeneity σμ2​1(1+αzαp)2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇1superscript1subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝2\sigma^{2}_{\mu}\frac{1}{(1+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}}. Finally, if private noise becomes large as αx→0→subscript𝛼𝑥0\alpha_{x}\rightarrow 0 and thus αz​(αx)→0→subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥0\alpha_{z}(\alpha_{x})\rightarrow 0, equilibrium multiplicity depends on prior dispersion, σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}, alone.111111This finding naturally differs from Angeletos and Werning, (2006), where σμ=0subscript𝜎𝜇0\sigma_{\mu}=0, such that noisy private signals unambiguously induce unique equilibria when public information is endogenous once αx→0→subscript𝛼𝑥0\alpha_{x}\rightarrow 0 and αz​(αx)→0→subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥0\alpha_{z}(\alpha_{x})\rightarrow 0. Related to this observation, we note that among all parameters, αx,αp,σμsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝜎𝜇\alpha_{x},\alpha_{p},\sigma_{\mu}, the prior’s dispersion σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu} is the only parameter which can ensure equilibrium uniqueness regardless of the values of the remaining parameters.

Concerning the different implications of heterogenous priors and heterogenous private signals, the key insight is that the endogeneity of public information inverts the original findings of Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004), Metz, (2002), and Hellwig, (2002), where increases in private information induce equilibrium uniqueness as in (9), where the public signal’s precision is exogenous. The same is not true for the role of prior dispersion, which is, contrary to the private signal’s dispersion, robust to the introduction of an endogenous public price signal and unambiguously contributes towards equilibrium uniqueness. That is, heterogeneity in priors, unlike heterogeneity in private information, provides a robust anchor for unique equilibria.

3.1 Observing Other’s Actions

In this section, agents can observe the size of the aggregate attack through a noisy public signal S=Φ−1​(A)+σε​ε𝑆superscriptΦ1𝐴subscript𝜎𝜀𝜀S=\Phi^{-1}(A)+\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon where ε∼𝒩​(0,1)similar-to𝜀𝒩01\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1). While games where agents can observe each other’s actions were already studied by Minelli and Polemarchakis, (2003), the current signal specification is taken from Dasgupta, (2007) and Angeletos and Werning, (2006), since it allows to preserve normal distributions. The following discussion of equilibrium uniqueness is accordingly parallel to that in Angeletos and Werning, (2006); and their results obtain as special cases where priors are uninformative, i.e., where αp=0subscript𝛼𝑝0\alpha_{p}=0. Compared to the analysis in Angeletos and Werning, (2006) we note that public signals of high precision can ensure unique threshold equilibria in our specification if priors are informative. That is, the role of the public signal in Angeletos and Werning, (2006), pp. 1733-1734, depends critically on the absence of an informative prior.121212More precisely, for a priorless game, Angeletos and Werning, (2006) show that threshold equilibria are always unique, but there may exist multiple equilibria in “strategies”. In the present model, which includes informative (possibly unique) priors, we show that multiple threshold equilibria may exist. However, if the public signal, over others’ actions A𝐴A, is sufficiently precise then threshold equilibria are always unique. The observation that public signals of high quality can ensure unique rather than multiple threshold equilibria is of interest in comparison with Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004), Metz, (2002), and Hellwig, (2002), who show that multiple threshold equilibria emerge once public signals are of high quality.

Compared to the previous two signal environments, signal S𝑆S carries two types of information. First, similar to signals (8) and (10), the signal S𝑆S allows agents to make inference on the true fundamental θ𝜃\theta since A=A​(θ,ψ∗)𝐴𝐴𝜃superscript𝜓A=A(\theta,\psi^{*}). Second, unlike signals (8) and (10), the particular signal realization S𝑆S is endogenous in the sense that S𝑆S is implicitly defined by S=Φ−1​(A​(θ,ψ∗​(S)))+σε​ε𝑆superscriptΦ1𝐴𝜃superscript𝜓𝑆subscript𝜎𝜀𝜀S=\Phi^{-1}(A(\theta,\psi^{*}(S)))+\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon. And there may exist multiple signal values S𝑆S for every given pair θ,ε𝜃𝜀\theta,\varepsilon. We examine these potential sources of multiplicity, namely equilibrium multiplicity in thresholds θ∗,ψ∗superscript𝜃superscript𝜓\theta^{*},\psi^{*} and equilibrium multiplicity in strategies S,ψ∗​(S)𝑆superscript𝜓𝑆S,\psi^{*}(S) in separate steps.

1) Equilibrium Multiplicity in Thresholds

In the augmented game, with heterogeneous priors, where agents observe the aggregate attack through signal S𝑆S, we have:

S=Φ−1​(A)+σε​ε,ε∼𝒩​(0,1)formulae-sequence𝑆superscriptΦ1𝐴subscript𝜎𝜀𝜀similar-to𝜀𝒩01\displaystyle S=\Phi^{-1}(A)+\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon,\quad\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1) (12)
A≡P​(ψ≤ψ∗​(S)|θ∗)=θ∗𝐴𝑃𝜓conditionalsuperscript𝜓𝑆superscript𝜃superscript𝜃\displaystyle A\equiv P(\psi\leq\psi^{*}(S)|\theta^{*})=\theta^{*} (13)
P​(θ≤θ∗|ψ∗,S)=c.𝑃𝜃conditionalsuperscript𝜃superscript𝜓𝑆𝑐\displaystyle P(\theta\leq\theta^{*}|\psi^{*},S)=c. (14)

Evaluation of (12)-(14) yields:

Proposition 4.

For every given signal realization S, threshold equilibria θ∗,ψ∗superscript𝜃superscript𝜓\theta^{*},\psi^{*} are unique if 12​π≤(αx+αz)αx​(αxαp)2+σμ212𝜋subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\leq\frac{(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})}{\alpha_{x}}\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}}\Big{)}^{2}+\sigma_{\mu}^{2}} where αz=αx2​αψσε2​α2=αx2σε2​(αx+αp2​σμ2)subscript𝛼𝑧superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2subscript𝛼𝜓subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜀superscript𝛼2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\alpha_{z}=\frac{\alpha_{x}^{2}\alpha_{\psi}}{\sigma^{2}_{\varepsilon}\alpha^{2}}=\frac{\alpha_{x}^{2}}{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}^{2}\sigma_{\mu}^{2})}. And equilibria are unique when priors are either sufficiently dispersed or when the public signal is sufficiently precise.

Proof.

See Appendix C ∎

With regard to the role of prior dispersion, Proposition 4 shows that prior dispersion contributes towards equilibrium uniqueness in the generalized setting where agents can observe each other’s actions.131313Note that increases in the prior’s dispersion reduce the public signal’s precision αz=αx2​αψσε2​α2=αx2σε2​(αx+αp2​σμ2)subscript𝛼𝑧superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2subscript𝛼𝜓subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜀superscript𝛼2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\alpha_{z}=\frac{\alpha_{x}^{2}\alpha_{\psi}}{\sigma^{2}_{\varepsilon}\alpha^{2}}=\frac{\alpha_{x}^{2}}{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}^{2}\sigma_{\mu}^{2})}; for intermediate values of σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}, it is therefore not necessarily true that increases in σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu} contribute towards uniqueness. The more significant finding, however, is that the public signal’s precision induces equilibrium uniqueness rather than multiplicity. That is, in the present framework, we find that the public signal allows agents to coordinate on one particular equilibrium rather than multiple equilibria as in Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004), Metz, (2002), Hellwig, (2002), and Angeletos and Werning, (2006). Moreover, the comparative statics with regard to the public signal carry over to the unique prior economy where σμ=0subscript𝜎𝜇0\sigma_{\mu}=0. Finally, for an uninformative prior where αp=0subscript𝛼𝑝0\alpha_{p}=0, we find that the uniqueness result of Angeletos and Werning, (2006) obtains as a special case.141414In a setting with a uniform uninformative prior, Angeletos and Werning, (2006), pp. 1733-1734, prove that threshold equilibria are always unique irrespective of the precisions αzsubscript𝛼𝑧\alpha_{z} and αxsubscript𝛼𝑥\alpha_{x}. To obtain this result, one can either repeat the calculations in Appendix C with αp=0subscript𝛼𝑝0\alpha_{p}=0. Alternatively, one can observe that the uniqueness condition in Proposition 4 is always satisfied once a sufficiently small value αpsubscript𝛼𝑝\alpha_{p} is chosen.

2) Equilibrium Multiplicity in Strategies

In this paragraph, we study the uniqueness of the equilibrium with respect to the signal S𝑆S. In Appendix C, we show that signal S𝑆S is, in equilibrium, equivalent to a signal Z​(S)=ααx​ψ∗​(S)−ααx​1αψ​S=θ+αpαx​E​[μ]−σε​ααx​1αψ​ε𝑍𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥superscript𝜓𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓𝑆𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑥𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscript𝜎𝜀𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓𝜀Z(S)=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\psi^{*}(S)-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}S=\theta+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha_{x}}E[\mu]-\sigma_{\varepsilon}\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}\varepsilon. And multiple equilibria can emerge in the sense151515At this point we do not discuss the conceptual validity of this alternative type/source of equilibrium multiplicity, which implies that the particular signal S is endogenous, i.e., depends on the ψ∗superscript𝜓\psi^{*} chosen. Angeletos and Werning, (2006), p.1730, provide a brief discussion and further references regarding this fundamental problem. that there may exist several signal values S𝑆S, and thus several values ψ∗​(S)superscript𝜓𝑆\psi^{*}(S), which satisfy Z​(S)=Z¯𝑍𝑆¯𝑍Z(S)=\bar{Z}. Concerning this potential source of equilibrium multiplicity we note

Proposition 5.

Equilibria in strategies ψ∗​(S)superscript𝜓𝑆\psi^{*}(S) are unique if 12​π≤(αxαp+αz)2+σμ21+αzαp12𝜋superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇21subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\leq\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}}\Big{)}^{2}+\frac{\sigma_{\mu}^{2}}{1+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{p}}}}, with αz=αx2σε2​(αx+αp2​σμ2)subscript𝛼𝑧superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\alpha_{z}=\frac{\alpha_{x}^{2}}{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}^{2}\sigma_{\mu}^{2})}. In the limit, where σμ→∞→subscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}\rightarrow\infty there exists a unique equilibrium. In the limit where αz→∞→subscript𝛼𝑧\alpha_{z}\rightarrow\infty, there exist multiple equilibria in strategies.

Proof.

See Appendix C ∎

Comparison of propositions 4 and 5 with regard to the prior’s dispersion yields an important corollary:

Corollary 3.

The overall equilibrium is unique in the limit where σμ→∞→subscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}\rightarrow\infty.

Corollary 3 underscores the main result of the paper, i.e., it confirms that sufficiently dispersed priors ensure unique equilibria. The public signal’s precision has a more differentiated influence on equilibria: it ensures uniqueness in thresholds if it is sufficiently precise, but at the same time it opens the door to multiple equilibria in strategies.

4 Conclusion

We have introduced heterogenous priors into the canonic global games model of Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004), Metz, (2002), and Hellwig, (2002). The analysis of the baseline model indicates that heterogeneity in priors, unlike heterogeneity in private signals, makes it more difficult for agents to coordinate on multiple equilibria. That is, the origins of belief heterogeneity are of crucial importance to the global games approach: heterogeneity in beliefs, which originates from the variance σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu} in prior expectations, contributes to equilibrium uniqueness. Dispersion in private signals, on the contrary, induces equilibrium multiplicity. In general, the prior’s dispersion can ensure unique equilibria as it “thins-out” the group of agents who hold “moderate” beliefs. That is, it reduces the mass of agents with moderate beliefs, and it is this group which can potentially coordinate on multiple equilibria. Equivalently, our results indicate that if player’s disagreement, as measured by σμsubscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}, is substantial, then they play unique equilibria.

More precisely, we find that if prior dispersion is small, (σμ<12​π)subscript𝜎𝜇12𝜋(\sigma_{\mu}<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}), equilibrium multiplicity depends on the relative precisions of private information (αx)subscript𝛼𝑥(\alpha_{x}) and the subjective prior (αp)subscript𝛼𝑝(\alpha_{p}). If priors are sufficiently dispersed, (σμ>12​π)subscript𝜎𝜇12𝜋(\sigma_{\mu}>\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}), equilibria are unique irrespective of the relative weights that players assign to private signals and priors. If prior dispersion (σμ)subscript𝜎𝜇(\sigma_{\mu}) vanishes, the original uniqueness condition for the common prior economy (αxαp≥12​π)subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝12𝜋(\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}) obtains.

To compare the implications of prior dispersion and dispersion in private information, we have discussed a modified game in which a financial market aggregates private information into a public price signal. Such a modified environment inverts the original findings of Morris and Shin, (2000, 2004), Metz, (2002), and Hellwig, (2002): increases in private information now induce equilibrium multiplicity instead of uniqueness. The same is not true for the role of prior dispersion, which is robust to such a change in the modelling environment and contributes unambiguously towards equilibrium uniqueness. Put differently, the extended model indicates that prior dispersion, rather than arbitrarily precise private information, anchors unique equilibria reliably.

In general, we found that sufficiently dispersed priors ensure unique equilibria across all three public signal environments. Regarding these public signals, it turned out that their implications in themselves varied significantly from case to case: increases in the public signal’s precision ensure multiple threshold equilibria in the first two environments, where signals only contain information on the unknown fundamental. The opposite can be true in the third environment, where public signals allow agents to observe each other’s actions. If such signals are of high quality, they can enable agents to coordinate on one unique threshold equilibrium.

Unlike previous studies, which have introduced heterogenous priors into the global games framework, we have given explicit conditions in terms of means and variances, which allow to study equilibrium multiplicity for a large economy at and away from the private information limit. That is, the present framework facilitates comparative statics in the information structure itself, which allows to characterize and compare the different implications of belief heterogeneity which originate from priors and private signals, respectively. Moreover, these comparative statics are useful in those applications of the global games framework where it is interesting, or necessary, to study the interaction of private and public information away from the limit where private signals are infinitely precise.

Appendix A Game with Exogenous Public Information

In this appendix, we derive the uniqueness condition that obtains once we augment our baseline model of Section 2 with a public signal:

Z=θ+σz​ε,ε∼𝒩​(0,1).formulae-sequence𝑍𝜃subscript𝜎𝑧𝜀similar-to𝜀𝒩01\displaystyle Z=\theta+\sigma_{z}\varepsilon,\quad\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1). (15)

This signal allows agents to improve their forecast of the probability with which the aggregate attack overwhelms the status quo. The modified payoff indifference condition therefore reads:

P​(θ≤θ∗|x∗,μ,Z)=c⇔Φ​(α​(θ∗−αxα​x∗−αpα​μ−αzα​Z))=c;α=αx+αp+αz,formulae-sequence𝑃𝜃conditionalsuperscript𝜃superscript𝑥𝜇𝑍𝑐⇔formulae-sequenceΦ𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼superscript𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝜇subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼𝑍𝑐𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧\displaystyle P(\theta\leq\theta^{*}|x^{*},\mu,Z)=c\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha}(\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}x^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\mu-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha}Z)\Big{)}=c;\quad\alpha=\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}, (16)

where Φ​()Φ\Phi() is the cumulative of the standard normal distribution. Again, we define ψ≡αxα​x+αpα​μ𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝜇\psi\equiv\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}x+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\mu and rewrite (16) as:

Φ​(α​(θ∗−ψ∗−αzα​Z))=c;α=αx+αp+αz.formulae-sequenceΦ𝛼superscript𝜃superscript𝜓subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼𝑍𝑐𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧\displaystyle\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha}(\theta^{*}-\psi^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha}Z)\Big{)}=c;\quad\alpha=\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}. (17)

The PIC in (17) locates a critical ψ∗superscript𝜓\psi^{*} such that agents attack if ψ>ψ∗𝜓superscript𝜓\psi>\psi^{*} and do not attack if ψ<ψ∗𝜓superscript𝜓\psi<\psi^{*}. To calculate the mass of attacking agents, we note that ψ|θ∼𝒩​(αxα​θ+αpα​E​[μ],(α2αx+αp2​σμ2)−1)similar-toconditional𝜓𝜃𝒩subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇superscriptsuperscript𝛼2subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇1\psi|\theta\sim\mathcal{N}(\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu],(\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}})^{-1}). Once we define αψ≡α2αx+αp2​σμ2subscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝛼2subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇\alpha_{\psi}\equiv\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}, the CMC can be written as:

P​(ψ​<ψ∗|​θ∗)=θ∗⇔Φ​(αψ​(ψ∗−αxα​θ∗−αpα​E​[μ]))=θ∗.formulae-sequence𝑃𝜓brasuperscript𝜓superscript𝜃superscript𝜃⇔Φsubscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇superscript𝜃\displaystyle P(\psi<\psi^{*}|\theta^{*})=\theta^{*}\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}(\psi^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu])\Big{)}=\theta^{*}. (18)

Substitution of (17) into (18) again yields a one-dimensional equation in the threshold level θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*}:

Φ​(αψ​(αz+αpα​θ∗−αzα​Z−αpα​E​[μ]−Φ−1​(c)​1α))=θ∗.Φsubscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼𝑍subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇superscriptΦ1𝑐1𝛼superscript𝜃\displaystyle\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}(\frac{\alpha_{z}+\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha}Z-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu]-\Phi^{-1}(c)\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}})\Big{)}=\theta^{*}. (19)

Accordingly, equilibria are unique if:161616Recall that αψ≡α2αx+αp2​σμ2subscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝛼2subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇\alpha_{\psi}\equiv\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}.

αψ​αz+αpα​12​π≦1⇔(αxαp+αz)2+σμ2​1(1+αzαp)2≥12​πformulae-sequencesubscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼12𝜋1⇔superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇1superscript1subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝212𝜋\displaystyle\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}\frac{\alpha_{z}+\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\leqq 1\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}}\Big{)}^{2}+\sigma^{2}_{\mu}\frac{1}{(1+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{p}})^{2}}}\geq\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} (20)

which is what we needed to show.

Appendix B Financial Market and Information Aggregation

In this appendix, we present a financial market that aggregates and reveals dispersed private information, on the fundamental θ𝜃\theta, through the stock price. For the present purpose, it is convenient to pick a special case of the linear CARA-normal noise trader equilibrium discussed by Grossman and Stiglitz, (1976, 1980), Hellwig, (1980), and Angeletos and Werning, (2006). Stocks are traded at a market price P𝑃P and pay an unknown amount θ𝜃\theta, which represents the firm’s fundamental strength. This market price will, in equilibrium, aggregate dispersed private information and reveal the true fundamental θ𝜃\theta partially. Where the partial revelation is due to aggregate noise-trader activity, σε​εsubscript𝜎𝜀𝜀\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon, ε∼𝒩​(0,1)similar-to𝜀𝒩01\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1), on the asset’s supply side. To characterize the market price signal, we proceed in three steps. First, we guess that there exists a linear price function, P=η1​θ+η2​ε+c𝑃subscript𝜂1𝜃subscript𝜂2𝜀𝑐P=\eta_{1}\theta+\eta_{2}\varepsilon+c. Regarding θ𝜃\theta, this function is informationally equivalent to a signal Z≡P−cη1=θ+η2η1​ε𝑍𝑃𝑐subscript𝜂1𝜃subscript𝜂2subscript𝜂1𝜀Z\equiv\frac{P-c}{\eta_{1}}=\theta+\frac{\eta_{2}}{\eta_{1}}\varepsilon, which reveals the true fundamental with precision αz=η12η22subscript𝛼𝑧subscriptsuperscript𝜂21subscriptsuperscript𝜂22\alpha_{z}=\frac{\eta^{2}_{1}}{\eta^{2}_{2}}. Second, given this price function, we characterize individual demands based on the information x,μ,Z𝑥𝜇𝑍x,\mu,Z, and calculate the market equilibrium. Finally, we determine the ratio η12η22subscriptsuperscript𝜂21subscriptsuperscript𝜂22\frac{\eta^{2}_{1}}{\eta^{2}_{2}} as αx2​1γ2​σε2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥21superscript𝛾2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜀\alpha_{x}^{2}\frac{1}{\gamma^{2}\sigma^{2}_{\varepsilon}}. That is, price signal Z𝑍Z indeed carries information αz=αx2​1γ2​σε2subscript𝛼𝑧superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥21superscript𝛾2subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜀\alpha_{z}=\alpha_{x}^{2}\frac{1}{\gamma^{2}\sigma^{2}_{\varepsilon}} as claimed in (10) in the main text.

1) Demand

Agents choose their optimal demands kisubscript𝑘𝑖k_{i} for the asset to maximize expected CARA utility:

kisubscript𝑘𝑖\displaystyle k_{i} =arg⁡maxki​{𝔼​[−e−γ​(θ−P)​ki|xi,μ,Z]}absentsubscript𝑘𝑖𝔼delimited-[]conditionalsuperscript𝑒𝛾𝜃𝑃subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑥𝑖𝜇𝑍\displaystyle=\underset{k_{i}}{\arg\max}\{\mathbb{E}[-e^{-\gamma(\theta-P)k_{i}}|x_{i},\mu,Z]\}
=arg⁡maxki​{γ​𝔼​[(θ−P)​ki|xi,μ,Z]−γ22​V​a​r​[(θ−P)​ki|xi,μ,Z]}absentsubscript𝑘𝑖𝛾𝔼delimited-[]conditional𝜃𝑃subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑥𝑖𝜇𝑍superscript𝛾22𝑉𝑎𝑟delimited-[]conditional𝜃𝑃subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑥𝑖𝜇𝑍\displaystyle=\underset{k_{i}}{\arg\max}\{\gamma\mathbb{E}[(\theta-P)k_{i}|x_{i},\mu,Z]-\frac{\gamma^{2}}{2}Var[(\theta-P)k_{i}|x_{i},\mu,Z]\}
=arg⁡maxki​{γ​(αxα​xi+αpα​μ+αzα​Z−P)​ki−γ22​ki2​1α},α=αx+αp+αzformulae-sequenceabsentsubscript𝑘𝑖𝛾subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼subscript𝑥𝑖subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝜇subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼𝑍𝑃subscript𝑘𝑖superscript𝛾22superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑖21𝛼𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧\displaystyle=\underset{k_{i}}{\arg\max}\{\gamma(\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}x_{i}+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\mu+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha}Z-P)k_{i}-\frac{\gamma^{2}}{2}k_{i}^{2}\frac{1}{\alpha}\},\quad\alpha=\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}

and the individual demand function writes:

kid=αxα​xi+αpα​μ+αzα​Z−Pα−1​γ.superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑖𝑑subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼subscript𝑥𝑖subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝜇subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼𝑍𝑃superscript𝛼1𝛾\displaystyle k_{i}^{d}=\frac{\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}x_{i}+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\mu+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha}Z-P}{\alpha^{-1}\gamma}. (21)

2) Equilibrium

Aggregate supply KS=σε​εsuperscript𝐾𝑆subscript𝜎𝜀𝜀K^{S}=\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon, is unobservable and distorted by noise-trader activity ε∼𝒩​(0,1)similar-to𝜀𝒩01\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1). From (21), we find that aggregate demand KDsuperscript𝐾𝐷K^{D} is:

KD=∫[0,1]∫ki​(μ)​ϕ​(μ)​𝑑μ​𝑑i=αxα​θ+αpα​E​[μ]+αzα​Z−Pα−1​γ.superscript𝐾𝐷subscript01subscript𝑘𝑖𝜇italic-ϕ𝜇differential-d𝜇differential-d𝑖subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼𝑍𝑃superscript𝛼1𝛾\displaystyle K^{D}=\int_{[0,1]}\int k_{i}(\mu)\phi(\mu)d\mu di=\frac{\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu]+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha}Z-P}{\alpha^{-1}\gamma}. (22)

Equilibrium requires that:

KD=KS⇔αxα​θ+αpα​E​[μ]+αzα​Z−Pα−1​γ=σε​ε.formulae-sequencesuperscript𝐾𝐷superscript𝐾𝑆⇔subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼𝑍𝑃superscript𝛼1𝛾subscript𝜎𝜀𝜀\displaystyle K^{D}=K^{S}\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\frac{\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu]+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha}Z-P}{\alpha^{-1}\gamma}=\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon. (23)

To close the argument, we now resubstitute Z=P−cη1𝑍𝑃𝑐subscript𝜂1Z=\frac{P-c}{\eta_{1}} and calculate the ratio η1η2subscript𝜂1subscript𝜂2\frac{\eta_{1}}{\eta_{2}}. First, we solve (23) for P𝑃P to obtain:

P=αxα−αzη1​θ−γ​σεα−αzη1​ε+αp​E​[μ]−αz​cη1α−αzη1.𝑃subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝜂1𝜃𝛾subscript𝜎𝜀𝛼subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝜂1𝜀subscript𝛼𝑝𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscript𝛼𝑧𝑐subscript𝜂1𝛼subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝜂1\displaystyle P=\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\eta_{1}}}\theta-\frac{\gamma\sigma_{\varepsilon}}{\alpha-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\eta_{1}}}\varepsilon+\frac{\alpha_{p}E[\mu]-\alpha_{z}\frac{c}{\eta_{1}}}{\alpha-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\eta_{1}}}. (24)

Comparison of (24) with our initial guess, P=η1​θ+η2​ε+c𝑃subscript𝜂1𝜃subscript𝜂2𝜀𝑐P=\eta_{1}\theta+\eta_{2}\varepsilon+c, indicates that η1,η2subscript𝜂1subscript𝜂2\eta_{1},\eta_{2} must satisfy:

η1=αxα−αzη1,η2=−γ​σεα−αzη1;α=αx+αp+αz.formulae-sequencesubscript𝜂1subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝜂1formulae-sequencesubscript𝜂2𝛾subscript𝜎𝜀𝛼subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝜂1𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧\displaystyle\eta_{1}=\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\eta_{1}}},\quad\eta_{2}=-\frac{\gamma\sigma_{\varepsilon}}{\alpha-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\eta_{1}}};\quad\alpha=\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}. (25)

We quickly determine η1=αx+αzαsubscript𝜂1subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼\eta_{1}=\frac{\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z}}{\alpha}, η2=−(αx+αz)​αzαsubscript𝜂2subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼\eta_{2}=-\frac{(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})\sqrt{\alpha_{z}}}{\alpha} to calculate η1η2=−αx​1γ​σεsubscript𝜂1subscript𝜂2subscript𝛼𝑥1𝛾subscript𝜎𝜀\frac{\eta_{1}}{\eta_{2}}=-\alpha_{x}\frac{1}{\gamma\sigma_{\varepsilon}}. At the same time, it follows from the definition of Z that Z=P−cη1=θ+η2η1​ε𝑍𝑃𝑐subscript𝜂1𝜃subscript𝜂2subscript𝜂1𝜀Z=\frac{P-c}{\eta_{1}}=\theta+\frac{\eta_{2}}{\eta_{1}}\varepsilon. Hence, agents who observe P𝑃P, and know the model’s coefficients, receive a signal Z=θ−αx−1​γ​σε​ε𝑍𝜃superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥1𝛾subscript𝜎𝜀𝜀Z=\theta-\alpha_{x}^{-1}\gamma\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon, as claimed in (10) in the main text.

Appendix C Proof of Propositions 4 and 5

In this appendix, we start by laying out the equations that describe equilibria. In turn, we characterize the possible equilibria described in propositions 4 and 5 in two separate paragraphs.

We recall the model from the main text

S=Φ−1​(A)+σε​ε,ε∼𝒩​(0,1)formulae-sequence𝑆superscriptΦ1𝐴subscript𝜎𝜀𝜀similar-to𝜀𝒩01\displaystyle S=\Phi^{-1}(A)+\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon,\quad\varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1) (26)
A≡P​(ψ≤ψ∗​(S)|θ)=θ𝐴𝑃𝜓conditionalsuperscript𝜓𝑆𝜃𝜃\displaystyle A\equiv P(\psi\leq\psi^{*}(S)|\theta)=\theta (27)
P​(θ≤θ∗|x,μ,S)=c𝑃𝜃conditionalsuperscript𝜃𝑥𝜇𝑆𝑐\displaystyle P(\theta\leq\theta^{*}|x,\mu,S)=c (28)

To calculate equilibria, we recall that agents act on x=θ+σx​ξ𝑥𝜃subscript𝜎𝑥𝜉x=\theta+\sigma_{x}\xi, with ξ∼𝒩​(0,1)similar-to𝜉𝒩01\xi\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1) and θ|μ∼𝒩​(μ,σp2)similar-toconditional𝜃𝜇𝒩𝜇superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑝2\theta|\mu\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma_{p}^{2}), where the prior μ𝜇\mu is distributed over the population as μ∼𝒩​(E​[μ],σμ)similar-to𝜇𝒩𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscript𝜎𝜇\mu\sim\mathcal{N}(E[\mu],\sigma_{\mu}). Moreover, we define ψ≡αxα​x+αpα​μ𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝜇\psi\equiv\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}x+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\mu with α=αx+αp+αz𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧\alpha=\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}. The PIC (28) now writes as:

Φ​(α​(θ∗−ψ∗−αzα​Z))=c;α=αx+αp+αz.formulae-sequenceΦ𝛼superscript𝜃superscript𝜓subscript𝛼𝑧𝛼𝑍𝑐𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧\displaystyle\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha}(\theta^{*}-\psi^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha}Z)\Big{)}=c;\quad\alpha=\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}. (29)

Again, (29) defines a critical ψ∗​(Z)superscript𝜓𝑍\psi^{*}(Z) such that agents attack if ψ≤ψ∗𝜓superscript𝜓\psi\leq\psi^{*} and do not attack if ψ>ψ∗𝜓superscript𝜓\psi>\psi^{*}. To calculate the mass of attacking agents, we note that ψ|θ∼𝒩​(αxα​θ+αpα​E​[μ],(α2αx+αp2​σμ2)−1)similar-toconditional𝜓𝜃𝒩subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇superscriptsuperscript𝛼2subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇1\psi|\theta\sim\mathcal{N}(\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu],(\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}})^{-1}). Once we define αψ≡α2αx+αp2​σμ2subscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝛼2subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇\alpha_{\psi}\equiv\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}, the CMC (27) can be written as:

A=P​(ψ​<ψ∗|​θ∗)=θ∗⇔Φ​(αψ​(ψ∗−αxα​θ∗−αpα​E​[μ]))=θ∗.formulae-sequence𝐴𝑃𝜓brasuperscript𝜓superscript𝜃superscript𝜃⇔Φsubscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇superscript𝜃\displaystyle A=P(\psi<\psi^{*}|\theta^{*})=\theta^{*}\quad\Leftrightarrow\quad\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}(\psi^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu])\Big{)}=\theta^{*}. (30)

Using this expression for the aggregate attack A𝐴A in condition (30), we can return to the public signal S𝑆S in (26) and write:

S=αψ​(ψ∗−αxα​θ−αpα​E​[μ])+σε​ε.𝑆subscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscript𝜎𝜀𝜀\displaystyle S=\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}(\psi^{*}-\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta-\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu])+\sigma_{\varepsilon}\varepsilon. (31)

Where S𝑆S in (31) is informationally equivalent to a signal

Z​(S)≡ααx​ψ∗​(S)−ααx​1αψ​S=θ+αpαx​E​[μ]−σε​ααx​1αψ​ε.𝑍𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥superscript𝜓𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓𝑆𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑥𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscript𝜎𝜀𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓𝜀\displaystyle Z(S)\equiv\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\psi^{*}(S)-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}S=\theta+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha_{x}}E[\mu]-\sigma_{\varepsilon}\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}\varepsilon. (32)

Regarding (32), we note that Z​(S)𝑍𝑆Z(S) contains two aspects (i) Z𝑍Z is a noisy public signal which reveals the true state of the economy θ𝜃\theta with precision αz=αx2​αψσε2​α2=αx2σε2​(αx+αp2​σμ2)subscript𝛼𝑧superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2subscript𝛼𝜓subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜀superscript𝛼2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\alpha_{z}=\frac{\alpha_{x}^{2}\alpha_{\psi}}{\sigma^{2}_{\varepsilon}\alpha^{2}}=\frac{\alpha_{x}^{2}}{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}^{2}\sigma_{\mu}^{2})} and (ii) the signal S𝑆S allows agents to align their strategies ψ∗​(S)superscript𝜓𝑆\psi^{*}(S). That is, for every given Z¯¯𝑍\bar{Z}, there may be several S𝑆S such that Z​(S)=Z¯𝑍𝑆¯𝑍Z(S)=\bar{Z}. That is, there is a potential source of equilibrium multiplicity, concerning S𝑆S, to which we turn in Paragraph 2) of this appendix. For now, we take S𝑆S as given and study the threshold equilibria θ∗​(S),ψ∗​(S)superscript𝜃𝑆superscript𝜓𝑆\theta^{*}(S),\psi^{*}(S).

1) Proof of Proposition 4: Multiplicity in Thresholds θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*}

For every given signal S𝑆S, we rewrite (30) as:

ψ∗=Φ−1​(θ∗)​1αψ+αxα​θ∗+αpα​E​[μ].superscript𝜓superscriptΦ1superscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇\displaystyle\psi^{*}=\Phi^{-1}(\theta^{*})\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}+\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}+\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}E[\mu]. (33)

To obtain an equation in θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*} only, we substitute Z​(S)=ααx​ψ∗​(S)−ααx​1αψ​S𝑍𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥superscript𝜓𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓𝑆Z(S)=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\psi^{*}(S)-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}S and (33) into (29). Rearranging then yields:

Φ​(α​(αpα​θ∗−1αψ​αx+αzαx​Φ−1​(θ∗)−(αx+αz)​αpαx​α​E​[μ]+αzαx​1αψ​S))=cΦ𝛼subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼superscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptΦ1superscript𝜃subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼𝐸delimited-[]𝜇subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓𝑆𝑐\displaystyle\Phi\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha}(\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}\theta^{*}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}\frac{\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{x}}\Phi^{-1}(\theta^{*})-\frac{(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})\alpha_{p}}{\alpha_{x}\alpha}E[\mu]+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}S)\Big{)}=c (34)

To derive the uniqueness condition, which ensures that there exist only one θ∗​(S)superscript𝜃𝑆\theta^{*}(S) for every given signal S𝑆S, we differentiate (34) with respect to θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*}:171717Note that for y=Φ−1​(θ∗)𝑦superscriptΦ1superscript𝜃y=\Phi^{-1}(\theta^{*}), we have d​θ∗d​y=ϕ​(y)𝑑superscript𝜃𝑑𝑦italic-ϕ𝑦\frac{d\theta^{*}}{dy}=\phi(y) and thus d​yd​θ∗=1ϕ​(y)=1ϕ​(Φ−1​(θ∗))𝑑𝑦𝑑superscript𝜃1italic-ϕ𝑦1italic-ϕsuperscriptΦ1superscript𝜃\frac{dy}{d\theta^{*}}=\frac{1}{\phi(y)}=\frac{1}{\phi(\Phi^{-1}(\theta^{*}))}.

ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)≤(αx+αz)αx​(αxαp)2+σμ2,italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\displaystyle\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})\leq\frac{(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})}{\alpha_{x}}\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}}\Big{)}^{2}+\sigma_{\mu}^{2}}, (35)

and hence, threshold equilibria are always unique iff 12​π≤(αx+αz)αx​(αxαp)2+σμ212𝜋subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\leq\frac{(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})}{\alpha_{x}}\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}}\Big{)}^{2}+\sigma_{\mu}^{2}}. Otherwise, if 12​π≥(αx+αz)αx​(αxαp)2+σμ212𝜋subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\geq\frac{(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})}{\alpha_{x}}\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}}\Big{)}^{2}+\sigma_{\mu}^{2}}, there may exist up to three threshold equilibria
θ1∗​(S),ψ1∗​(S);θ2∗​(S),ψ2∗​(S);θ3∗​(S),ψ3∗​(S)subscriptsuperscript𝜃1𝑆superscriptsubscript𝜓1𝑆subscriptsuperscript𝜃2𝑆superscriptsubscript𝜓2𝑆subscriptsuperscript𝜃3𝑆superscriptsubscript𝜓3𝑆\theta^{*}_{1}(S),\psi_{1}^{*}(S);\theta^{*}_{2}(S),\psi_{2}^{*}(S);\theta^{*}_{3}(S),\psi_{3}^{*}(S) for every given signal value S𝑆S.

2) Proof of Proposition 5: Multiplicity in Strategies ψ∗​(S)superscript𝜓𝑆\psi^{*}(S)

To preclude multiple solutions181818The existence of at least one solution is ensured. It follows from (33) that l​i​mS→∞​Φ​(θ∗​(S))−1S𝑙𝑖subscript𝑚→𝑆Φsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑆1𝑆lim_{S\rightarrow\infty}\frac{\Phi(\theta^{*}(S))^{-1}}{S} and l​i​mS→−∞​Φ​(θ∗​(S))−1S𝑙𝑖subscript𝑚→𝑆Φsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃𝑆1𝑆lim_{S\rightarrow-\infty}\frac{\Phi(\theta^{*}(S))^{-1}}{S} are constants. Rewriting Z​(S)=ααx​ψ∗​(S)−ααx​1αψ​S𝑍𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥superscript𝜓𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓𝑆Z(S)=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\psi^{*}(S)-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}S as Z​(S)=ααx​S​(ψ∗​(S)S−ααx​1αψ)𝑍𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥𝑆superscript𝜓𝑆𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓Z(S)=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}S(\frac{\psi^{*}(S)}{S}-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}) and recalling ψ​(θ∗​(S))𝜓superscript𝜃𝑆\psi(\theta^{*}(S)) as given in (33) one can show that Z​(S)𝑍𝑆Z(S) varies with S𝑆S between ∞\infty and −∞-\infty. S​(Z¯)𝑆¯𝑍S(\bar{Z}) to the equation Z​(S)=Z¯𝑍𝑆¯𝑍Z(S)=\bar{Z}, where Z​(S)=ααx​ψ∗​(S)−ααx​1αψ​S𝑍𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥superscript𝜓𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓𝑆Z(S)=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\psi^{*}(S)-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}S, it will suffice to show that ∂Z​(S)∂S|(32)=ααx​∂ψ∗∂S−ααx​1αψ≤0\frac{\partial Z(S)}{\partial S}_{|(\ref{s11})}=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{\partial\psi^{*}}{\partial S}-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}\leq 0. To calculate the derivative ∂ψ​(θ∗​(S))∂S=∂ψ∗∂θ∗​∂θ∗∂S𝜓superscript𝜃𝑆𝑆superscript𝜓superscript𝜃superscript𝜃𝑆\frac{\partial\psi(\theta^{*}(S))}{\partial S}=\frac{\partial\psi^{*}}{\partial\theta^{*}}\frac{\partial\theta^{*}}{\partial S}, defined by (33) and (34), we differentiate (34) which yields ∂θ∗∂S=−αzαx​1αψαpα−1αψ​αx+αzαx​1ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)superscript𝜃𝑆subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼1subscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥1italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1\frac{\partial\theta^{*}}{\partial S}=\frac{-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}}{\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}\frac{\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})}} and (33), (which is a 1:1:111:1 mapping between ψ∗superscript𝜓\psi^{*} and θ∗superscript𝜃\theta^{*}), to obtain ∂ψ∗∂θ∗=1αψ​ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)+αxαsuperscript𝜓superscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝜓italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼\frac{\partial\psi^{*}}{\partial\theta^{*}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})}+\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}. Hence, we have

∂Z​(S)∂S𝑍𝑆𝑆\displaystyle\frac{\partial Z(S)}{\partial S} =ααx​∂ψ∗∂S−ααx​1αψ=ααx​∂ψ∗∂θ∗​∂θ∗∂S−ααx​1αψabsent𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥superscript𝜓𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥superscript𝜓superscript𝜃superscript𝜃𝑆𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓\displaystyle=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{\partial\psi^{*}}{\partial S}-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{\partial\psi^{*}}{\partial\theta^{*}}\frac{\partial\theta^{*}}{\partial S}-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}} (36)
=−ααx​1αψ⏟-+(1αψ​ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)+αxα)⏟+​−αzαx​1αψαpα−1αψ​αx+αzαx​1ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)⏟+⁣/⁣−​ααx.absent⏟𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓⏟1subscript𝛼𝜓italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑥𝛼⏟subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥1subscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑝𝛼1subscript𝛼𝜓subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥1italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1𝛼subscript𝛼𝑥\displaystyle=\underset{-}{\underbrace{-\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}}}+\underset{+}{\underbrace{\Big{(}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})}+\frac{\alpha_{x}}{\alpha}\Big{)}}}\underset{+/-}{\underbrace{\frac{-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}}{\frac{\alpha_{p}}{\alpha}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_{\psi}}}\frac{\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{x}}\frac{1}{\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})}}}}\frac{\alpha}{\alpha_{x}}.

Once we recall that αψ≡α2αx+αp2​σμ2subscript𝛼𝜓superscript𝛼2subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇\alpha_{\psi}\equiv\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}, rearranging (36) gives:

∂Z​(S)∂S𝑍𝑆𝑆\displaystyle\frac{\partial Z(S)}{\partial S} =−αx+αp2​σμ2αx+(αx+αp2​σμ2+αx​ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1))​−αzαxϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)​αp​αxαx+αp2​σμ2−(αx+αz)absentsubscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇subscript𝛼𝑥italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑥italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧\displaystyle=-\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}}{\alpha_{x}}+\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}+\alpha_{x}\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})\Big{)}\frac{-\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{x}}}{\frac{\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})\alpha_{p}\alpha_{x}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}}-(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})} (37)
=1αx​[−αx+αp2​σμ2+(αx+αp2​σμ2+αx​ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1))​−αzϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)​αp​αxαx+αp2​σμ2−(αx+αz)]absent1subscript𝛼𝑥delimited-[]subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇subscript𝛼𝑥italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑧italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧\displaystyle=\frac{1}{\alpha_{x}}\Big{[}-\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}+\Big{(}\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}+\alpha_{x}\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})\Big{)}\frac{-\alpha_{z}}{\frac{\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})\alpha_{p}\alpha_{x}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}}-(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})}\Big{]}
=1αx​[−αx​(αp+αz)​ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)+αx​αx+αp2​σμ2]​1ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)​αp​αxαx+αp2​σμ2−(αx+αz)absent1subscript𝛼𝑥delimited-[]subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇1italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧\displaystyle=\frac{1}{\alpha_{x}}\Big{[}-\alpha_{x}(\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z})\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})+\alpha_{x}\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}\Big{]}\frac{1}{\frac{\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})\alpha_{p}\alpha_{x}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}}-(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})}
=[−(αp+αz)​ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)+αx+αp2​σμ2]​1ϕ​(Φ​(θ∗)−1)​αp​αxαx+αp2​σμ2−(αx+αz)absentdelimited-[]subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇1italic-ϕΦsuperscriptsuperscript𝜃1subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑥subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑝subscriptsuperscript𝜎2𝜇subscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧\displaystyle=\Big{[}-(\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z})\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})+\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}\Big{]}\frac{1}{\frac{\phi(\Phi(\theta^{*})^{-1})\alpha_{p}\alpha_{x}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}+\alpha^{2}_{p}\sigma^{2}_{\mu}}}-(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})}

From (37), and the fact that θ∗∈(0,1)superscript𝜃01\theta^{*}\in(0,1), it follows that equilibria in strategies are unique if

12​π≤(αxαp+αz)2+σμ21+αzαp,αz=αx2σε2​(αx+αp2​σμ2)formulae-sequence12𝜋superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇21subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\displaystyle\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\leq\sqrt{\Big{(}\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{x}}}{\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z}}\Big{)}^{2}+\frac{\sigma_{\mu}^{2}}{1+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{p}}}},\quad\alpha_{z}=\frac{\alpha_{x}^{2}}{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}^{2}\sigma_{\mu}^{2})} (38)

and

12​π≤(αx+αz)2αx​αp2+σμ2​(αx+αz)2αx2.12𝜋superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2\displaystyle\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\leq\sqrt{\frac{(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})^{2}}{\alpha_{x}\alpha_{p}^{2}}+\frac{\sigma_{\mu}^{2}(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})^{2}}{\alpha_{x}^{2}}}. (39)

That is, once (38) and (39) hold, we have ∂Z​(S)∂S<0𝑍𝑆𝑆0\frac{\partial Z(S)}{\partial S}<0, which ensures unique solutions S​(Z¯)𝑆¯𝑍S(\bar{Z}) to the equation Z​(S)=Z¯𝑍𝑆¯𝑍Z(S)=\bar{Z}. Comparison shows that inequality (39) is less restrictive than (38).191919αx(αp+αz)2+σμ21+αzαp≤(αx+αz)2αx​αp2+σμ2​(αx+αz)2αx2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇21subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2\sqrt{\frac{\alpha_{x}}{(\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z})^{2}}+\frac{\sigma_{\mu}^{2}}{1+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{p}}}}\leq\sqrt{\frac{(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})^{2}}{\alpha_{x}\alpha_{p}^{2}}+\frac{\sigma_{\mu}^{2}(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})^{2}}{\alpha_{x}^{2}}} follows from the inequalities αx(αp+αz)2≤(αx+αz)2αx​αp2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝subscript𝛼𝑧2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝2\frac{\alpha_{x}}{(\alpha_{p}+\alpha_{z})^{2}}\leq\frac{(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})^{2}}{\alpha_{x}\alpha_{p}^{2}} and σμ21+αzαp≤σμ2​(αx+αz)2αx2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇21subscript𝛼𝑧subscript𝛼𝑝superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥subscript𝛼𝑧2superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2\frac{\sigma_{\mu}^{2}}{1+\frac{\alpha_{z}}{\alpha_{p}}}\leq\frac{\sigma_{\mu}^{2}(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{z})^{2}}{\alpha_{x}^{2}}, which are easy to verify. Evaluation of (38) therefore yields:

  1. 1.

    In the limit where σμ→∞→subscript𝜎𝜇\sigma_{\mu}\rightarrow\infty, equilibria in strategies are unique.

  2. 2.

    In the limit202020Note that the public signal’s precision is endogenous, i.e., given by αz=αx2σε2​(αx+αp2​σμ2)subscript𝛼𝑧superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑥2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝛼𝑥superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑝2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜇2\alpha_{z}=\frac{\alpha_{x}^{2}}{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}(\alpha_{x}+\alpha_{p}^{2}\sigma_{\mu}^{2})}.
